description: A palm nut soup from the Niger Delta — rich, orange, fragrant with banga spices that exist in no other Nigerian soup. Completely different from every other soup in this library. Pairs with starch, eba, or white rice.

ingredients.name: palm fruits (fresh or canned)
ingredients.quantity: 1.5kg fresh or 800ml canned palm nut cream
ingredients.note: Fresh palm fruits give the best flavour — boil, pound, and extract the cream yourself. Canned palm nut cream (tinned oroma) is a perfectly acceptable shortcut and cuts the prep time significantly.

ingredients.name: beef or goat meat
ingredients.note: Goat meat is traditional in banga soup and handles the bold spices beautifully. Beef works well too.

ingredients.name: stockfish (okporoko)
ingredients.note: Soak 30 minutes. Debone. Important for depth.

ingredients.name: dried banga spice (oburunbebe stick)
ingredients.note: This is the defining flavour of banga soup — a dried bark with a deeply aromatic, slightly medicinal, irreplaceable taste. Available in Nigerian grocery stores. No substitute exists.

ingredients.name: banga leaves (obenetete / atama leaves)
ingredients.quantity: 1 tbsp dried or a small handful fresh
ingredients.note: Dried banga leaves available at Nigerian stores. Adds a distinctive herbal note specific to this soup. Skip if completely unavailable — the soup is still good without it.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Blend or pound. Banga soup is moderately spiced — 2 is the sweet spot.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 for parboiling meat, 1 chopped for the soup.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: Adds the savoury backbone underneath the banga spices.

ingredients.name: locust beans (iru/dawadawa)
ingredients.note: Rinse briefly. Works well with the palm nut base.

ingredients.name: dried uziza leaves
ingredients.note: A peppery, aromatic leaf added at the end. Pairs beautifully with banga. Skip if unavailable.

ingredients.name: meat stock
ingredients.note: From parboiling the meat. Used to adjust consistency.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season at the end — the palm cream and fish contribute salt.

steps.title: Extract Palm Nut Cream (if using fresh)
steps.instruction: If using fresh palm fruits, begin here — this step takes the most time and defines the quality of the entire soup. Wash the palm fruits thoroughly under cold running water to remove any debris, then place them in a large pot and cover completely with water. Boil over high heat for 30–40 minutes until the outer orange pulp is completely soft — you should be able to squeeze a fruit easily between your fingers without resistance. Drain the boiled fruits, then pound them in batches in a mortar, adding warm water gradually to help loosen the pulp from the fibres and central nuts. Squeeze and press the pounded mixture through a sieve or cheesecloth to extract the thick orange cream, discarding the dry fibres and hard inner nuts. If using canned palm nut cream, simply open, stir well to recombine any separated oil, and proceed directly to step 2.
micro_steps: Wash 1.5kg of fresh palm fruits thoroughly under cold running water, removing any leaves or debris
Place all the palm fruits in a large pot and pour in enough cold water to fully submerge them
Bring to a rolling boil over high heat — about 10 minutes
Reduce to a steady boil and cook for 30–40 minutes
After 30 minutes, squeeze a fruit firmly between your fingers — if the orange pulp separates from the nut easily, it is ready; if not, continue boiling for another 10 minutes
Drain the water from the pot and allow the fruits to cool for 5 minutes — cool enough to handle
Working in batches, add palm fruits to a mortar and pound firmly, rotating as you go
Add a splash of warm water every few strikes to help the pulp release from the fibres
Continue pounding until the pulp is visibly separated from the fibres and central nuts
Squeeze handfuls of the pounded mixture over a sieve or cheesecloth set over a large bowl — press and squeeze firmly to extract all the cream
Discard the dry fibres and hard inner nuts
The extracted cream should be thick, deeply orange, and coat the back of a spoon — set aside
prep_notes: If using canned palm nut cream, skip this step entirely — open, stir well to recombine separated oil, and start at step 2
Start the stockfish soaking at least 30 minutes before you begin — put it in cold water as the very first thing you do
Have your mortar and a large sieve or cheesecloth ready before boiling the palm fruits — you will need them while the fruits are still warm
Parboil and fry the meat in parallel with the palm fruit extraction to save time — they do not need your full attention simultaneously
steps.sensory_cue: Freshly extracted palm nut cream should be thick, deeply orange, and smell gently sweet and slightly fruity — like fresh palm oil with a floral edge. It should coat the back of a spoon heavily and leave an orange stain. If it looks thin or pale, you have not squeezed enough — press the fibres again.
steps.failure_risk: Under-boiling the palm fruits makes extraction nearly impossible — the pulp clings to the fibres and will not release no matter how hard you pound. The fruits must be completely, uniformly soft all the way through before you start pounding. When in doubt, give them another 10 minutes.

steps.title: Prepare Proteins and Stock
steps.instruction: Season the meat generously with salt, a crushed seasoning cube, and roughly chopped onion, then parboil in minimal water until about 75% tender — you want concentrated, richly flavoured stock, so resist the urge to add a lot of water. Reserve every drop of this stock. Fry the parboiled meat in hot oil until deeply golden-brown on the outside. While the meat cooks, drain and carefully debone the pre-soaked stockfish, keeping the pieces as large as possible. Debone the smoked fish and break it into large flakes. Blend or pound the scotch bonnet peppers separately and set aside. Have everything organised and ready before the palm cream goes on the heat.
micro_steps: Season 1kg of beef or goat meat with a generous pinch of salt, 1 crushed seasoning cube, and 1 roughly chopped onion
Place in a pot with just enough water to barely cover — about 2–3cm above the meat
Bring to a boil then reduce to medium — cook beef for 30–35 minutes, goat for 50–60 minutes
Test tenderness: a fork should meet slight resistance but penetrate without force
Remove meat from pot and reserve all the stock in a bowl — do not discard
Heat a generous splash of oil in a frying pan over high heat until shimmering
Fry the parboiled meat in batches until deeply golden-brown — 3–4 minutes per side
Remove and set aside on a plate
Drain the stockfish from its soaking water, pull apart carefully, and remove all bones — keep in large pieces
Run your fingers through the smoked fish to find and remove all bones, then break into large flakes
Blend or pound 2–3 scotch bonnet peppers until smooth — set aside in a small bowl
steps.sensory_cue: Fried meat should be deeply golden-brown with a slightly crisp exterior. The reserved stock should be dark, rich, and smell strongly savoury — this is the backbone of the soup's depth. All proteins should be fully deboned and ready to add without any further prep.
steps.failure_risk: Goat meat takes significantly longer to become tender than beef — often 50–60 minutes of parboiling versus 30 for beef. If you pull goat meat too early, it will be tough in the finished soup and no amount of simmering in palm cream will fix it. Confirm tenderness with a fork before frying.

steps.title: Cook the Palm Nut Cream Base
steps.instruction: Pour the palm nut cream into a large, heavy pot — this is now the base of the entire soup. Add 1 cup of the reserved meat stock and 1 cup of water to loosen it to a thick, pourable consistency. Bring to a boil over medium-high heat, stirring regularly to prevent the cream from scorching on the bottom. Once boiling, add the banga spice sticks or ground banga spice and the chopped onion. Stir everything together and let the palm cream cook at an active boil for 15–20 minutes, stirring every 3 minutes. The cream will reduce, deepen in colour, and the kitchen will gradually fill with one of the most distinctive aromas in Nigerian cooking — that is your signal that the banga spice is working.
micro_steps: Pour the extracted fresh palm cream or the full contents of the canned palm nut cream into a large pot
Add 1 cup of the reserved meat stock and stir to combine
Add 1 cup of water and stir — the mixture should look like a thick, pourable orange liquid
Place over medium-high heat and bring to a boil, stirring frequently to prevent sticking
Once boiling, add 2 oburunbebe sticks or 1 tbsp of ground banga spice
Add the second onion, roughly chopped, and stir in
Maintain an active boil over medium-high heat
Stir every 3 minutes, scraping the bottom of the pot to prevent scorching
Cook for 15–20 minutes — the cream will visibly reduce and deepen from bright orange to a richer, darker orange
After 15 minutes, check: the surface should show the natural palm oil beginning to separate and rise — this means it is cooking correctly
steps.sensory_cue: Cooking palm cream with banga spice produces one of the most distinctive aromas in all of Nigerian cooking — deeply aromatic, slightly woody, sweet, and complex, unlike anything else. The cream should reduce to a thick, deeply orange soup base and you should see natural palm oil beginning to float on the surface. When the kitchen smells unmistakably of banga, the base is ready.
steps.failure_risk: Insufficient cooking of the palm cream at this stage leaves it tasting raw, flat, and oily in an unpleasant way. It needs to actively boil and reduce for the full 15–20 minutes before anything else goes in. The oil visibly separating and rising to the surface is your reliable sign that the base has cooked long enough.

steps.title: Add Crayfish, Aromatics and Proteins
steps.instruction: With the palm cream fully reduced and fragrant, reduce the heat to medium and begin building the layers. Add the ground crayfish first and stir it in — the colour and aroma will shift immediately. Add the locust beans if using, rinsed briefly, and the blended scotch bonnet pepper. Stir everything together and let it cook for 2 minutes before adding the proteins. Add the fried meat, stockfish, and flaked smoked fish in that order, stirring gently with a folding motion after each addition to coat everything in the orange base without breaking the fish apart. Add the crushed seasoning cubes, stir in, and bring the pot back to a gentle boil.
micro_steps: Reduce heat to medium once the palm cream base is fully reduced
Add 2 tablespoons of ground crayfish and stir in firmly — the soup will darken slightly and smell richer immediately
Rinse the locust beans briefly under cold water if using, then add to the pot and stir
Add the blended scotch bonnet pepper and stir in thoroughly
Let this cook together for 2 minutes, stirring once
Add the fried meat pieces and fold gently into the base — make sure every piece is coated
Add the deboned stockfish pieces and fold in carefully to keep them intact
Add the flaked smoked fish last and fold in very gently — smoked fish breaks apart easily
Crush 2 seasoning cubes into powder and stir into the soup
Bring the pot back to a gentle boil over medium heat and stir once from the bottom
steps.sensory_cue: The soup at this stage should look thick, deeply orange, and full of protein. The banga spice aroma should still be prominent but now layered with crayfish and smoked fish — a richer, more complex smell. Every piece of meat and fish should look coated in the orange base.
steps.failure_risk: If the soup looks noticeably thin at this point — watery rather than creamy — let it boil uncovered for 5 more minutes before adding the proteins. Adding meat and fish to a thin base dilutes it further and the finished soup will never thicken to the right consistency.

steps.title: Simmer, Season and Add Leaves
steps.instruction: Cover the pot and simmer on low heat for 20–25 minutes, lifting the lid to stir gently every 5 minutes. The soup will thicken further as it reduces and all the flavours knit together. Taste it now and add salt carefully — remember the smoked fish and stockfish have already contributed salt to the base. If using dried banga leaves, add them now and stir in, giving them at least 10 minutes in the heat to soften and release their flavour. If using dried uziza leaves, hold them back until the final 5 minutes only — they are peppery and strong and need very little cooking. Do one final taste before serving, adjusting salt if needed. The finished soup should taste unlike anything else you have cooked.
micro_steps: Cover the pot with a lid and reduce heat to low
Set a timer for 5 minutes, then lift the lid and stir gently from the bottom — do not scrape aggressively
Repeat the 5-minute stir cycle for a total of 20–25 minutes
After 15 minutes, taste the soup — assess the salt level before adding anything
Add salt in very small pinches, tasting between each addition
If using dried banga leaves, add them now — stir in and replace the lid for the remaining cook time
At the 20-minute mark, check consistency — the soup should coat a spoon heavily and look glossy
If using dried uziza leaves, add them in the final 5 minutes only — a small handful, shredded
Stir once after adding uziza and taste immediately — it should add a gentle peppery note, not dominate
Do a final taste — adjust salt one last time if needed
Turn off heat and serve immediately alongside starch, eba, or white rice
steps.sensory_cue: Finished banga soup is thick, deeply orange, and has a naturally glossy surface from the palm oil. The aroma should carry the banga spice clearly — aromatic, slightly sweet, and deeply savoury all at once. One taste should be unmistakably unlike any other Nigerian soup you have made; that distinctive, slightly medicinal-sweet depth from the oburunbebe is the signature and it should be present in every spoonful.
steps.failure_risk: Uziza leaves are intensely peppery and aromatic — a little goes a very long way. Add only a small handful, stir, and taste immediately before deciding to add more. Too much uziza overwhelms the delicate banga spice flavour that is the entire point of this soup, and it cannot be removed once added.
